text: What is a sulfhydryl group?
choices: -OH
-SH
-NH2
-COOH
explain: A sulfhydryl group contains a sulfur atom bonded to hydrogen (–SH).

text: What is a carbonyl group?
choices: A group containing a carbon atom double-bonded to an oxygen atom
A group containing phosphorus bonded to four oxygens
A group that always makes a molecule basic
A group found only in amino acids
explain: Carbonyl groups have a C=O double bond and are common in many biological molecules.

text: What is the difference between a monomer and a polymer?
choices: A monomer is larger than a polymer
A monomer is a building block, while a polymer is a larger molecule made from repeating monomers
A monomer contains only carbon, while a polymer contains many elements
A monomer can exist only in carbohydrates
explain: Monomers are small subunits that join to form polymers (large molecules made of repeating units).

text: Which force is weakest but can still help stabilize large biological molecules?
choices: Covalent bond
Ionic bond
Van der Waals interaction
Peptide bond
explain: Van der Waals interactions are individually weak but collectively contribute to molecular stability.

text: What is the main difference between saturated and unsaturated fatty acids?
choices: Saturated fatty acids contain nitrogen, and unsaturated fatty acids do not
Saturated fatty acids contain only single bonds, while unsaturated fatty acids contain one or more double bonds
Unsaturated fatty acids are always charged, while saturated fatty acids are neutral
Saturated fatty acids are found only in plants
explain: Saturated fats have only single C–C bonds; unsaturated fats contain C=C double bonds that introduce kinks.

text: Which statement best describes the role of R groups in amino acids?
choices: They determine the identity and chemical properties of different amino acids
They hold nucleotides together in DNA
They replace peptide bonds in proteins
They are identical in all amino acids
explain: R groups (side chains) give each amino acid its specific chemical properties and identity.

text: Which molecule is usually single-stranded?
choices: DNA
RNA
Cellulose
Triglyceride
explain: RNA is typically single-stranded and can fold into complex structures.

text: Which statement best describes hydrocarbon chains?
choices: They are usually nonpolar and hydrophobic
They are strongly charged and highly soluble in water
They are made of repeating amino acids
They form the sugar-phosphate backbone of DNA
explain: Hydrocarbon chains are largely nonpolar and do not interact favorably with water.

text: A scientist mutates a protein so that two cysteine residues are replaced by alanine residues. Which outcome is most likely?
choices: The protein may lose stability because it can no longer form the same disulfide bonds
The protein will gain a phosphate backbone
The mutation will automatically make the protein longer
The protein will become RNA
explain: Cysteines can form disulfide bonds that stabilize folding; alanine cannot form those covalent links.

text: A researcher compares two sugars with the same molecular formula. Sugar X is part of a structural polysaccharide, while Sugar Y is readily used in cellular respiration. Which explanation is best?
choices: The two sugars may be isomers with different structures and therefore different biological roles
One sugar must contain no oxygen
Structural sugars are always proteins
Cellular respiration uses only lipids, not sugars
explain: Isomeric differences (linkage/orientation) can change function despite identical formulas.

text: A molecule contains several hydroxyl groups and dissolves readily in water. What is the best explanation?
choices: Hydroxyl groups increase polarity and allow favorable interactions with water
Hydroxyl groups make all molecules nonpolar
The molecule must be a lipid because it dissolves in water
Water dissolves only molecules with peptide bonds
explain: Hydroxyl groups enable hydrogen bonding with water, increasing solubility.

text: A biologist studies a membrane protein with many nonpolar amino acids exposed on the outside of the protein. In which environment is this protein most likely found?
choices: Floating freely in the cytoplasm
Embedded in the nonpolar interior of a membrane
Dissolved in blood plasma as a fully water-soluble protein
Attached only to DNA in the nucleus
explain: Nonpolar exterior residues are consistent with a protein embedded within the membrane's hydrophobic interior.

text: A polymer is broken into monomers in a reaction that consumes water. Which statement is correct?
choices: The reaction is hydrolysis, and water is used to break covalent bonds
The reaction is dehydration synthesis, and water is produced to break bonds
The reaction must involve amino acids only
The reaction cannot occur in cells
explain: Hydrolysis splits polymers by adding water to break covalent bonds; it is common in cells.

text: A point mutation changes a DNA codon so that the resulting amino acid is much larger than the original. The amino acid lies in a tightly packed region of the protein. Why might this matter?
choices: The larger side chain could interfere with proper folding or packing, altering protein function
The protein will automatically gain more peptide bonds
Protein size can never affect structure
The DNA must now become single-stranded RNA
explain: A bulkier residue in a tight core can disrupt packing and folding, impairing function.

text: A triglyceride and a phospholipid both contain fatty acids, but only one forms bilayers in water. Why?
choices: The phospholipid has a hydrophilic region and a hydrophobic region, allowing self-assembly in water
The triglyceride contains peptide bonds that prevent bilayer formation
The phospholipid is made of amino acids
The triglyceride has a phosphate backbone like DNA
explain: Phospholipids are amphipathic (polar head, nonpolar tails), which drives bilayer formation in aqueous environments.

text: A protein loses activity after a pH change, but analysis shows its amino acid sequence is unchanged. Which level of structure was most directly altered?
choices: Primary structure
Secondary, tertiary, or quaternary structure
The number of monomers in the protein
The phosphodiester bonds of the protein
explain: pH changes can disrupt ionic and hydrogen-bonding interactions that maintain higher-order folding and subunit assembly.

text: A DNA sample with higher G-C content is compared with a DNA sample with higher A-T content. Which prediction is most reasonable?
choices: The G-C rich DNA may be more stable because G-C pairs form more hydrogen bonds
The A-T rich DNA must contain ribose instead of deoxyribose
The G-C rich DNA cannot replicate
The A-T rich DNA is always single-stranded
explain: G-C pairs form three hydrogen bonds (vs. two for A-T), generally increasing duplex stability.

text: A scientist replaces several hydroxyl-containing side chains on the surface of a protein with nonpolar side chains. Which outcome is most likely in a watery environment?
choices: The protein may become less soluble or less stable in water
The protein will become a carbohydrate
The protein will lose all covalent bonds
The protein must now bind DNA
explain: Removing polar groups from the surface reduces favorable water interactions and can decrease solubility/stability.

text:
Students perform Benedict’s test on four unknown samples and record the following:

Sample 1: remains blue
Sample 2: turns green
Sample 3: turns orange
Sample 4: turns brick red

Which conclusion is most reasonable?
choices: Sample 4 likely contains the greatest amount of reducing sugar
Sample 1 likely contains the greatest amount of reducing sugar
Sample 2 must contain the most protein because it changed color
Sample 3 must be a lipid because it changed to orange
explain: Stronger color change toward orange/red in Benedict's test indicates higher reducing sugar concentration.

text: A class tests two enzymes that digest carbohydrates. Enzyme A breaks down starch efficiently but has almost no effect on cellulose. Which explanation best fits the results?
choices: Enzyme A recognizes specific bond types or substrate shapes present in starch but not cellulose
Starch and cellulose are identical in every relevant way
Cellulose is made of amino acids rather than sugars
Enzyme A works only at high temperature
explain: Enzyme specificity depends on substrate structure and type of glycosidic linkage; starch and cellulose differ in linkage orientation.

text: Researchers compare two enzymes that differ by one amino acid. Enzyme X functions normally, while Enzyme Y has a much lower reaction rate. Both enzymes are the same length and remain mostly folded. Which hypothesis is best?
choices: The amino acid change likely altered the active site or another region important for catalysis
Enzyme Y must have lost all peptide bonds
Enzyme X is actually DNA
Enzyme Y must now be a lipid
explain: A single substitution can impair catalytic residues or substrate binding without global unfolding.

text:
A lab group studies pH buffering. They add equal amounts of base to two solutions.

Solution A: pH changes from 7.2 to 7.4
Solution B: pH changes from 7.2 to 9.1

Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Solution A has stronger buffering capacity
Solution B has stronger buffering capacity
Solution A contains no hydrogen ions
Solution B must contain more protein
explain: Smaller pH change after adding base indicates better buffering capacity.

text: Students compare how two unknown molecules behave in water. Molecule P dissolves uniformly. Molecule Q forms a separate oily layer. Infrared analysis suggests Molecule Q contains long hydrocarbon chains and very few polar groups. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Molecule Q is likely hydrophobic because its structure is mostly nonpolar
Molecule P must be a triglyceride because it dissolved
Molecule Q is likely strongly ionic
Molecule P and Q must be isomers because both contain carbon
explain: Long hydrocarbon chains indicate nonpolarity and poor interaction with water, producing phase separation.

text: A scientist measures melting behavior of two lipid samples. Sample X melts at a lower temperature than Sample Y. Chemical analysis shows Sample X has more carbon-carbon double bonds. Which explanation best fits the evidence?
choices: Double bonds create bends that reduce tight packing among the fatty acid chains
Double bonds increase peptide bonding between lipid molecules
Double bonds make the lipid more like DNA
Double bonds always make molecules acidic
explain: Kinks from double bonds reduce van der Waals packing and lower melting temperature.

text: A mutation changes one nucleotide in a DNA coding region. The new amino acid in the protein has the opposite charge of the original. The protein now binds its target poorly. Which interpretation is most reasonable?
choices: The charge change likely disrupted interactions needed for binding or proper folding
The mutation must have increased the protein’s length
DNA changes cannot affect protein binding
The protein must have been converted into RNA
explain: Changing charge at a binding interface can alter electrostatic interactions and disrupt binding.

text:
Students analyze an unknown biomolecule and find the following:
- contains carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen
- is built from monomers with variable side chains
- loses function when its three-dimensional shape changes

Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The biomolecule is most likely a protein
The biomolecule is most likely a nucleic acid
The biomolecule is most likely a polysaccharide
The biomolecule is most likely a triglyceride
explain: These characteristics fit proteins made of amino acids with variable R groups and structure-dependent function.

text: A researcher places phospholipids into water and observes spontaneous formation of vesicles. Which explanation best supports this observation?
choices: The amphipathic structure of phospholipids causes hydrophilic heads to face water and hydrophobic tails to avoid it
Phospholipids form peptide bonds with water
Water becomes nonpolar around phospholipids
Phospholipids polymerize into nucleic acids in water
explain: Amphipathic phospholipids spontaneously assemble into bilayers or vesicles to minimize unfavorable tail–water interactions.

text: A teacher shows students two related proteins from different species. The proteins have very similar amino acid sequences and very similar functions. Which idea does this most directly support?
choices: Similar primary structure often leads to similar higher-level structure and function
Protein function is unrelated to amino acid sequence
All proteins with similar function must have identical sequence
Proteins and nucleic acids are the same type of molecule
explain: Similar sequences commonly fold similarly and perform similar functions, reflecting evolutionary conservation.
